primary.authors: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C

questions.verifiedExplanation: The Berlin criteria for ARDS require: (1) Acute onset within 1 week of a known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms; (2) Bilateral opacities on chest imaging (CXR or CT) not fully explained by effusions, atelectasis, or nodules; (3) Respiratory failure NOT fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (may require echocardiography to exclude); (4) Hypoxemia classified by PaO2/FiO2 ratio on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O — Mild: 200-300, Moderate: 100-200, Severe: ≤100. An elevated PCWP >18 mmHg would suggest cardiogenic pulmonary edema, NOT ARDS. The Berlin criteria replaced the older AECC definition and specifically eliminated the requirement for PCWP measurement, instead requiring that respiratory failure not be 'fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload.' ARDS and heart failure can coexist, but PCWP >18 as a standalone criterion is NOT part of the Berlin definition — it is the OPPOSITE of what Berlin requires.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: ARDS is defined by acute onset, bilateral lung infiltrates on chest radiography or CT scan of a non-cardiac origin, and PaO2/FiO2 ratio

questions.verifiedExplanation: The ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation protocol uses a target tidal volume of 6 mL/kg ideal body weight (IBW). StatPearls states the protocol sets 'tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight' with 6 mL/kg as the target. This strategy reduces ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) by minimizing volutrauma (overdistension of alveoli) and barotrauma (excessive airway pressures). The landmark ARMA trial (NEJM 2000) demonstrated a 22% relative reduction in mortality with 6 mL/kg IBW vs 12 mL/kg IBW (31% vs 39.8%). Other key ARDSNet parameters: plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, respiratory rate up to 35, SpO2 88-95%, pH goal 7.30-7.45. CRITICAL: tidal volume is calculated using IDEAL body weight (based on height and sex), NOT actual body weight — obese patients receive the same tidal volume as non-obese patients of the same height.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW), respiratory rate up to 35 bpm, SpO2 88% to 95%, plateau pressure less than 30 cm H2O

questions.verifiedExplanation: Prone positioning in ARDS is indicated when the P/F ratio is <150 (moderate-to-severe ARDS) despite optimized ventilator settings. The PROSEVA trial (Guérin et al., NEJM 2013) demonstrated that prone positioning for ≥16 hours/day in patients with severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality from 32.8% to 16% (NNT = 6). StatPearls notes prone positioning 'has shown benefits in about 50% to 70% of patients' and improves oxygenation by recruiting dependent lung zones, improving diaphragmatic excursion, and increasing functional residual capacity. Prone positioning is NOT indicated for mild ARDS (P/F 200-300) or patients without ARDS (P/F >300). 'Any P/F ratio' is too broad — the evidence supports benefit specifically in moderate-severe disease.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Prone position has shown benefits in about 50% to 70% of patients. The improvement in oxygenation is rapid and allows a reduction in FiO2 and PEEP

questions.verifiedExplanation: Pneumonia is the #1 cause of ARDS, accounting for approximately 60% of cases (with or without sepsis). AAFP data confirms 'Most cases of ARDS in adults are associated with pneumonia with or without sepsis (60%) or with nonpulmonary sepsis (16%).' Other causes include aspiration, inhalation injury, pancreatitis, trauma, transfusion (TRALI), drug toxicity, and burns. Trauma is a recognized cause but less common than pneumonia. Pulmonary embolism can cause respiratory failure but is NOT a classic ARDS trigger (it causes V/Q mismatch, not diffuse alveolar damage). Heart failure causes cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which is explicitly EXCLUDED from the ARDS definition — ARDS is by definition non-cardiogenic.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: ARDS is an acute disorder...in the absence of any evidence of cardiogenic pulmonary edema

questions.verifiedExplanation: The FACTT trial (Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial, NEJM 2006) demonstrated that a conservative (restrictive) fluid strategy improves ventilator-free days and ICU-free days compared to a liberal fluid strategy in ARDS, without increasing non-pulmonary organ failures. Conservative fluid management aims to keep the patient 'dry' — achieving even to negative fluid balance using diuretics and fluid restriction. The rationale: ARDS involves increased pulmonary capillary permeability, so excess intravascular volume worsens pulmonary edema and impairs gas exchange. Liberal fluids would worsen pulmonary edema. There was no mortality difference at 60 days, but the significant improvement in ventilator-free days (14.6 vs 11.2 days) makes conservative strategy the standard of care. Colloids vs crystalloids was not the focus of this trial.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: careful management of fluids in high-risk patients can be helpful

questions.verifiedExplanation: A P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP ≥5 classifies as SEVERE ARDS per the Berlin criteria. The Berlin classification: Mild ARDS: P/F 200-300 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~27%); Moderate ARDS: P/F 100-200 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~35%); Severe ARDS: P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~45%). A P/F of 80 is well below the ≤100 threshold for severe ARDS. This patient requires lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW), high PEEP, prone positioning (≥16 hours/day), neuromuscular blockade consideration, and potentially ECMO if refractory. The PEEP requirement of ≥5 cmH2O is specified because some degree of positive pressure is needed to standardize the measurement — this patient on PEEP 10 clearly meets this criterion.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Berlin definition: PaO2/FiO2 categories of ≤100 (severe), 101-200 (moderate), and 201-300 (mild) on PEEP ≥5

questions.verifiedExplanation: ECMO (Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation) in ARDS is reserved for refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management — it is a rescue therapy, not first-line. ECMO is considered when patients remain severely hypoxemic (P/F <80 or <60 despite optimal ventilator settings, prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade, and high PEEP). The EOLIA trial (Combes et al., NEJM 2018) suggested a trend toward benefit in severe ARDS refractory to conventional management. VV-ECMO (veno-venous) provides gas exchange support while allowing ultra-lung-protective ventilation, reducing VILI. ECMO requires specialized centers and carries risks including hemorrhage, thrombosis, infection, and hemolysis. It is NOT first-line for severe ARDS (lung-protective ventilation + prone positioning are first), NOT for mild ARDS, and NOT for all moderate ARDS patients.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

questions.verifiedExplanation: Permissive hypercapnia means accepting higher-than-normal pCO2 levels in order to keep tidal volumes low and prevent ventilator-induced lung injury, provided the pH remains above 7.25 (some protocols accept ≥7.20). When using low tidal volumes (6 mL/kg IBW), CO2 clearance may be insufficient, leading to respiratory acidosis. Rather than increasing tidal volume (which would cause volutrauma in stiff ARDS lungs), the strategy 'permits' elevated pCO2 levels — prioritizing lung protection over normal blood gas values. The ARDSNet protocol targets pH 7.30-7.45 but allows respiratory rate increases up to 35 bpm to compensate before accepting hypercapnia. Low pCO2 with high tidal volumes would defeat the purpose of lung protection. 'Zero pCO2' is physiologically impossible. 'Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes' describes the pre-ARDSNet approach that caused more lung injury and higher mortality.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight, pH goal 7.30 to 7.45
